programs.name: South Dakota Medicaid for Seniors and Disabled

eligibility.income_limits: South Dakota is an income cap state. For long-term care Medicaid (Nursing Home Medicaid and Waivers), single applicants have a monthly income limit of $2,901; married couples (both applying) $5,802; married (one applying) $2,901 for the applicant with spousal allowances. Community spouse protections include Minimum Monthly Maintenance Needs Allowance $2,643.75–Maximum $4,066.50 and CSRA $32,532–$162,660[1][3][4].
eligibility.asset_limits: $2,000 for recipients (IRAs and retirement plans are countable). Home equity limit $752,000. Exemptions not detailed in sources but standard Medicaid exemptions (primary home under equity limit, one vehicle, personal belongings) typically apply. Strategies exist to qualify over limits via planning[1][3].
other_requirements: Nursing Home Level of Care (NHLOC) for Nursing Home Medicaid and Waivers; functional need for ADLs for other long-term services[1]
Medical need for long-term care; U.S. citizenship or qualified immigrant status; state residency[1][5]

benefits.value: Long-term care services including nursing home care, home/community-based services via waivers, doctor visits, hospital stays, prescriptions, home modifications (if unable to live safely at home independently). Covers costs whether at home, community, or care facility. No premiums, copays, coinsurance, or deductibles[1][5][7].

methods: Online: http://dss.sd.gov/applyonline[6]
Mail: Any Department of Social Services (DSS) Division of Economic Assistance office[6]
In-person: Local DSS office[6]
application.processing_time: 45 days for most applications; 90 days if disability determination required[5][6]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned in sources; may apply for specific waiver services[1]
forms: Applications available for download at DSS website; no specific form number listed[6]
documents: Proof of income, assets, residency, citizenship/immigration status, medical needs documentation for long-term care (e.g., NHLOC assessment), Social Security numbers, Medicare info if applicable[1][5]

geography.regional_variations: Processing at local DSS offices but no specific differences in benefits, providers, or wait times noted by region[6]
offices_or_providers: Local DSS Division of Economic Assistance offices statewide[6]

gotchas: Must have NHLOC for nursing home/waivers; functional ADL need for other services—people miss medical assessment requirement[1]
Income cap state: Excess income disqualifies unless planned around (e.g., spousal allowances, trusts); IRAs count as assets[1][3]
Home equity over $752,000 disqualifies long-term care unless exceptions; estate recovery applies (hardship waivers exist)[3]
Dual Medicare/Medicaid eligible possible but requires meeting Medicaid asset rules; apply via Medicare first if applicable[5]
Retroactive coverage up to 3 prior months if expenses and eligible then[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Income/asset limits strict for long-term care with spousal protections (CSRA, MMNA); requires medical NHLOC; statewide but local DSS processing; 2026 LTC income $2901 single[1][3][4]

programs.name: Home and Community-Based Options and Person Centered Excellence (HOPE) Waiver

eligibility.age: 65 or older, OR 18-64 with a qualifying disability from Social Security Administration[1][2]

income_limits.description: Income limit is 300% of the Federal Benefit Rate (FBR), which adjusts annually in January[3]
income_limits.married_couples: Each spouse evaluated individually; each allowed up to the monthly limit if both are applicants[1][3]
income_limits.spousal_income: For married couples where only one spouse applies, spousal income is not counted against the applicant[1]
income_limits.needs_allowance: Up to $3,160.50 per month of joint income can be allocated to the non-applicant spouse[1]

asset_limits.single_or_widowed: Up to $2,000 in countable assets (cash in checking/savings accounts and similar accessible resources)[1]
asset_limits.married_couples_both_applicants: Each spouse must meet the $2,000 limit individually[1]
asset_limits.married_couples_one_applicant: Applicant must have $2,000 or less; non-applicant spouse may have up to $126,420 in countable assets[1]
asset_limits.home_equity_exemption: Home is exempt if applicant lives in it or has intent to return; home equity interest cannot exceed $730,000 (2025)[3]
asset_limits.additional_exemptions: Home is also exempt if spouse lives in home, minor child (under 21) lives in home, or disabled/blind child (any age) lives in home[3]

other_requirements: Must be a South Dakota resident[6]
Must meet nursing facility level of care (NFLOC) as determined by standardized assessment[2][4]
Cannot currently reside in hospital, nursing facility, or ICF/MR setting[1]
Must participate in initial needs assessment[1][8]
Must receive at least one waiver service at least once per month[1]
Must have individual support plan prepared[2]
Must reside or will reside at home or HOPE waiver-approved home and community-based setting[2]

benefits.value: Home and community-based services for individuals who need nursing facility level of care[5]
benefits.varies_by: Individual need and support plan; specific hours/dollar amounts not detailed in search results
benefits.capacity_note: Program capped at 1,834 concurrent recipients statewide (as of 2019)[1]

methods: Phone: Dakota at Home (833) 663-9673[5]
Referral: Dakota at Home Referral for HOPE Waiver/State Plan Services[4]
application.waitlist: Not explicitly stated; program has statewide capacity cap of 1,834 concurrent recipients[1]
forms: Home Care Assessment (HCA) tool used to determine nursing facility level of care need[3]
documents: Call Dakota at Home for specific document requirements[5]
Income verification documents (likely required for income limit verification)
Asset documentation (likely required for asset limit verification)
Proof of South Dakota residency (likely required)
Medical/functional assessment documentation
application_process_steps: 1. Call Dakota at Home (833) 663-9673 to inquire about eligibility and begin process[5]
2. Participate in initial assessment (Home Care Assessment)[3][8]
3. Department determines if participant meets nursing facility level of care[4]
4. SD DSS Long Term Care Benefits Specialist determines financial eligibility[4]

geography.regional_variations: Not specified in available sources; program appears uniformly administered statewide
offices_or_providers: Administered through Long Term Service and Supports (LTSS)[5]
Providers must meet both LTSS and South Dakota Medicaid Provider Enrollment criteria[4]
Specific provider locations not listed in search results; contact Dakota at Home for provider availability in your area

programs.official_source: https://dss.sd.gov/ (South Dakota Department of Social Services); Medicaid.gov waiver listing: https://www.medicaid.gov/medicaid/section-1115-demo/demonstration-and-waiver-list/83171
gotchas: Program has a statewide capacity cap of 1,834 concurrent recipients[1] — availability may be limited and waitlists possible
Income and asset limits are strictly enforced; applying when over limits will result in denial[3]
Applicant must meet nursing facility level of care requirement — this is not automatic for elderly or disabled individuals; functional assessment required[3]
Must receive at least one waiver service monthly to maintain eligibility[1]
Home equity limit of $730,000 (2025) applies unless spouse, minor child, or disabled child lives in home[3]
Disabled individuals (ages 18-64) can continue receiving services under the aged category when they turn 65[3]
Waiver approval expires September 30, 2026[7] — renewal status should be verified
Income limits increase annually in January; 2025 limit of $2,901/month is not permanent[3]
For married couples, spousal income is not counted only if the spouse is not applying; if both apply, each is evaluated individually[1]
Non-applicant spouse can have significantly higher assets ($126,420) than applicant ($2,000), creating planning opportunities[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: This program's eligibility is primarily income and asset-tested with a functional (nursing facility level of care) requirement. Benefits are service-based rather than cash-based, with specific services determined by individual support plan. The program operates statewide with a fixed capacity cap. Income limits adjust annually. Home equity is treated as exempt asset under specific conditions. The program requires ongoing participation in assessments and monthly service receipt to maintain eligibility.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Before calling Dakota at Home**, verify that your loved one meets the **nursing facility level of care** requirement — this is the most common barrier. Simply being elderly or disabled is not sufficient; they must need the level of care typically provided in a nursing facility.[3]

**Income and asset limits are strict.** The 2025 income limit is $2,901/month for single applicants.[3] If your loved one exceeds this, they will be denied. However, for married couples, the non-applicant spouse can have up to $126,420 in assets while the applicant must stay under $2,000 — this creates planning opportunities.[1]

**The program is capped at 1,834 concurrent recipients statewide,[1]** which means availability may be limited. Contact Dakota at Home immediately at (833) 663-9673 to understand current wait times and availability in your area.

**Services are individualized** based on the support plan, not a fixed benefit amount. The program covers in-home services, respite care, meals, adult day services, and assisted living options — but the specific combination and hours depend on assessed need.[5]

**The waiver approval expires September 30, 2026,[7]** so verify current program status before making long-term plans.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits for PACE enrollment itself; financial eligibility applies only for full Medicaid coverage to avoid private pay. For South Dakota Medicaid long-term care (relevant for dual-eligible coverage), income must be under 300% of the Federal Benefit Rate ($2,901/month in 2025 for an individual; varies by household size per state Medicaid rules). No specific South Dakota PACE income table available as program not yet implemented statewide[2][5].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits for PACE enrollment. For Medicaid coverage, assets $2,000 or less for an individual (excludes primary home, one vehicle, personal belongings, burial plots; countable assets include bank accounts, second vehicles, investments)[2].
other_requirements: Live in the service area of a PACE organization (none currently operational statewide in South Dakota)
Certified by South Dakota as needing nursing home level of care (requires extensive assistance with 2+ activities of daily living like bathing, dressing, eating)
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services support
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prescription drug plan, hospice, or certain other programs
Voluntary enrollment; average participant is 76 with multiple chronic conditions, 90% dual Medicare-Medicaid eligible[1][5][6]

benefits.value: All-inclusive: primary and specialty medical care, prescription drugs, hospitalization, nursing home care when needed, adult day health center, home care, transportation, meals/nutrition, therapy (physical/occupational/speech), social services, caregiver support; no copays/deductibles once enrolled. Provided via interdisciplinary team at PACE center; scope exceeds traditional Medicare/Medicaid in flexibility and coordination[1][3][5].

methods: No active PACE providers in South Dakota; contact South Dakota Department of Human Services (DHS) for updates on implementation (general LTSS line not specified in results; feasibility study ongoing)[5]

Process (generalized, state-specific when implemented): Intake consultation at PACE center/home, in-home assessment, nursing facility level of care determination, Medicaid eligibility check if applicable, care plan development[5]
application.processing_time: Varies by state and provider; not specified for South Dakota (generalized process from intake to enrollment)
application.waitlist: Potential waitlists depend on provider capacity; none currently as no programs operational[5]
forms: State-specific Medicaid forms for level of care and eligibility determination; no PACE-specific form named for SD
documents: Proof of age (ID/birth certificate)
Proof of residency in service area
Medical records/assessments for nursing home level of care
Medicare/Medicaid cards if applicable
Income/asset documentation for Medicaid eligibility (bank statements, pension awards)
Physician referral or health history[1][2][5]

geography.restrictions: No operational PACE organizations in South Dakota; limited to service areas of PACE providers (none exist); feasibility study conducted for statewide implementation, but not yet available[5][6]
geography.regional_variations: Varies by PACE organization service area; no regional differences in SD as program not implemented
offices_or_providers: None operational; contact SD DHS for future providers post-feasibility study[5]

programs.official_source: https://dhs.sd.gov (South Dakota Department of Human Services; see LTSS provider portal for PACE feasibility study)
gotchas: PACE not currently available anywhere in South Dakota—no providers operational despite feasibility study[5][6]
Must live in a PACE service area; moving out requires disenrollment
Nursing home level of care certification by state is strict—needs assessment proves inability to live safely without intensive support
Private pay option averages $4,000–$5,000/month if not Medicaid-eligible; no financial aid beyond that[2]
Cannot be in Medicare Advantage or hospice; disenrollment possible but regulated[1]
90% participants dual-eligible—check Medicaid first for free coverage[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: No operational programs in South Dakota (feasibility study phase only); eligibility not financially restricted but geographically limited to zero service areas; benefits coordinated via interdisciplinary team without caps; Medicaid asset/income rules apply indirectly for full coverage

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

eligibility.income_limits: South Dakota follows federal guidelines tied to FPL with state-specific dollar amounts that change annually. For 2025/2026 (estimates vary slightly by source): QMB (100% FPL): Individual ≤$1,275-$1,350/month, Couple ≤$1,724-$1,824/month. SLMB (120% FPL): Individual ≤$1,526/month, Couple ≤$2,064/month. QI (135% FPL): Individual ≤$1,715-$1,781/month, Couple ≤$2,320-$2,400/month. Limits include $20 disregard for some; must be Medicare Part A/B entitled. Full household table not specified beyond individual/couple—contact DSS for exact current figures as they adjust yearly.[1][2][3][4][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: Applies to all programs: Individual $9,430-$9,950 (some older sources cite $4,000); Couple $14,130-$14,910 (older $6,000). Counts: checking/savings, CDs. Exempt: primary home, one car, burial plots, irrevocable burial trusts, personal belongings. Exact exemptions per SD rules—verify with application.[1][3][4][6]
other_requirements: Must be entitled to/receiving Medicare Part A and B (except QDWI for Part A only).
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant.
Reside in South Dakota.
Meet countable income after disregards.

benefits.value: QMB: Pays Medicare Part A/B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, copays. SLMB/QI: Pays Part B premiums only. Automatic qualification for Extra Help (Part D low-income subsidy, e.g., ≤$12.65 copay/drug in 2026). No direct services—pure premium/cost coverage.[1][2][3][4]

methods: Mail/PDF form: Download 'Application for Medicare Savings Programs' from https://dss.sd.gov/formsandpubs/docs/MEDELGBLTY/270Medicaresavingsapplication.pdf[2]
Phone: Call SD DSS at 1-877-999-5612 (toll-free, TTY/TDD 1-877-486-2048) or local office for benefits specialist[6]
Online: Check eligibility/info at https://dss.sd.gov/medicaid/Eligibility/default.aspx; apply via DSS portal or SHIINE assistance[2][6]
In-person: Local DSS office or SHIINE volunteer (free application help via Senior Health Insurance Information & Education Program)[6]
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; typically 45 days for Medicaid-related apps—call DSS for current SD timeline.
application.waitlist: QI has first-come-first-served with priority to prior recipients; limited federal funding may create waitlist[4]
forms: Application for Medicare Savings Programs (form referenced in 270 PDF)[2]
documents: Social Security number (or immigrant document).
Proof of income (paystubs, benefit letters).
Proof of resources (bank statements).
Medicare card.
Proof of residency.

geography.regional_variations: Administered uniformly statewide by DSS; SHIINE volunteers available locally but no noted differences in eligibility/benefits/wait times by region[2][6]
offices_or_providers: Statewide DSS offices; local SHIINE counselors for assistance[6]

gotchas: Income/resource limits change yearly (e.g., 2025 vs 2026 figures differ)—always verify current with DSS.
QI requires annual reapplication; may have waitlist due to funding.
Conflicting resource limits in sources ($4k vs $9k)—use current federal/SD ($9k+ individual).
Must have Part A/B; QMB auto-triggers Extra Help but others may need separate LIS app.
Countable income uses Medicaid rules with disregards—gross income often disqualifies mistakenly.
Married couples use joint limits even if only one on Medicare.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)

eligibility.income_limits: For households with at least one member age 60 or older (elderly/disabled households) in South Dakota, there is **no gross income limit**; eligibility is based solely on the **net income test** (must be 100% or less of federal poverty guidelines). Standard household limits (Oct 1, 2025–Sept 30, 2026) for reference: |Household Size|Gross Monthly Income (130% FPL)|Net Monthly Income (100% FPL)| |1|$1,696|$1,305| |2|$2,292|$1,763| |3|$2,888|$2,221| |4|$3,483|$2,680| |5|$4,079|$3,138| |6|$4,675|$3,596| |7|$5,271|$4,055| |8|$5,867|$4,513| |Each additional|+ $596|+ $459|. Net income = gross income minus deductions (e.g., standard $209, medical expenses over $35 for elderly/disabled, shelter costs up to $744 cap for non-elderly/disabled households, utilities, child support). Social Security, pensions, VA benefits count as income.[3][2][1]
eligibility.asset_limits: Countable resources ≤ **$4,500** for households with elderly (60+) or disabled member ($3,000 otherwise). **Exempt:** primary home, one vehicle, most retirement savings. Bank accounts and cash count if over limit.[3]
other_requirements: South Dakota resident; U.S. citizen or certain legal non-citizens.
All household members need SSN (or apply for one).
Household includes those who live together and share food purchases/preparation.
Work requirements exempt for elderly 60+ (apply to able-bodied 16-59 unless exempt).

benefits.value: Monthly EBT card benefits for groceries (no cash). Amount based on household size, net income, deductions (e.g., example: 2-person elderly household with $1,200 gross might get $415/month after deductions). Max allotment scales by size; subtract 30% of net income from max.[5][3]

methods: Online: South Dakota Benefits Portal (apply via dss.sd.gov).
Phone: Contact local DSS office or state helpline (find via dss.sd.gov/economicassistance/snap.aspx).
Mail/In-person: Local Department of Social Services (DSS) offices statewide; download form from dss.sd.gov.
Multiple routes available.
application.processing_time: Typically 30 days; expedited if very low income (7 days). No specific SD waitlist mentioned.
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, Social Security award letters, pensions).
ID/SSN, proof of residency.
Medical expenses (for elderly/disabled deductions).
Rent/mortgage, utility bills, bank statements (for assets/deductions).

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide to eligible SD residents.
geography.regional_variations: Administered via local DSS offices; no benefit differences noted, possible processing variations by office/county.
offices_or_providers: Local DSS offices (locations at dss.sd.gov)

gotchas: Elderly households skip gross income test but must calculate net income accurately (deductions key: medical >$35, shelter).
Assets include bank accounts but exempt home/car; many miss retirement savings exemption.
Include all who share food in household, even non-elderly family.
South Dakota lacks Broad-Based Categorical Eligibility (BBCE), so stricter asset/income rules than some states.
Benefits recertify periodically (12 months in SD); report income changes only if rising above limits.
programs.data_shape_notes: Elderly/disabled households exempt from gross income test and higher asset limit ($4,500); net income calculated with elderly-specific deductions (uncapped shelter/medical); benefits scale by household size and net income.

programs.name: Low Income Energy Assistance Program (LIEAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Gross monthly household income must be at or below 200% of the federal poverty level, based on the most recent three months of income for all household members. Annual limits for 2025 (add $500 per additional person beyond 10): 1 Person: $18,650; 2 Person: $25,142; 3 Person: $31,634; 4 Person: $38,126; 5 Person: $44,618; 6 Person: $51,110; 7 Person: $22,514 (partial table from source, consistent with 200% FPL scaling); 8 Person: $23,014; 9 Person: $23,514; 10 Person: $24,431. Households where all members receive SNAP are automatically categorically income eligible.[1][2][5]
other_requirements: Household must be responsible for heating costs (paid directly to energy supplier or included in rent).
Everyone age 18+ in the household must sign the application.
Funding availability affects eligibility.

benefits.value: One-time supplemental payment for primary heating costs, paid directly to utility company. Amount based on household income, size, and fuel type. Related programs: Crisis (ECIP) for emergencies like shutoffs; Cooling: $1025 for eligible LIEAP households; Weatherization: energy efficiency upgrades (no fixed dollar amount, based on audit).[1][2][5]

methods: Online: https://www.sd.gov/cs?id=sc_cat_item&sys_id=a254bd6edbf7f410b2fb93d4f3961974
Mail: South Dakota Department of Social Services, Office of Energy Assistance, 700 Governors Drive, Pierre, SD 57501
Email: DSSHeat@state.sd.us (scan and send verifications)
application.processing_time: Not specified; apply early as funding is limited and may run out.
application.waitlist: Possible for Weatherization due to limited funds; not mentioned for core LIEAP.[5]
forms: Low Income Energy Assistance Application (online via SD.gov)
Vendor Claim Form (for energy suppliers, online submission)
documents: Proof of household income for the 3 calendar months prior to application month (for everyone in household).
Proof of home heating and electric costs (bills paid to supplier or as part of rent).

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide, but Weatherization uses regional community action programs.
geography.regional_variations: Weatherization completed by four community action programs serving specific counties (contact program for your county); no variations noted for core LIEAP heating assistance.[5]
offices_or_providers: Central office: 700 Governors Drive, Pierre, SD 57501
Weatherization: Four community action programs by county (details on DSS site)

gotchas: Heating assistance only available fall/winter; cooling not standard (separate $1025 program for prior LIEAP recipients).
Funding limited—apply early, applications may close if funds exhausted.
Crisis/ECIP requires income eligibility plus immediate crisis (e.g., shutoff notice).
Roommates sharing utility bill count as one household.[1]
Weatherization prioritizes elderly/disabled/families with children; renters need landlord permission.
programs.data_shape_notes: Income at exactly 200% FPL with 3-month lookback; no asset test; benefits as one-time payment varying by income/size/fuel; Weatherization county-based with priority tiers and waitlists; categorical eligibility via SNAP.

**Key Takeaway for Families with Elderly Loved Ones:**

Your elderly family member qualifies for **priority consideration** if their household income is at or below 200% of the federal poverty level and they own their home (or have landlord permission as a renter)[3]. However, priority status does not guarantee faster processing—funds are limited and waiting lists exist[2]. The program provides **free home improvements** (no cost to the homeowner) that typically save $372+ annually on energy bills[4]. The critical first step is contacting your county's Community Action Agency, which you can find by calling 1-800-233-8503 or visiting dss.sd.gov/weatherization[3][7]. Be prepared with 3 months of household income documentation and proof of home ownership or landlord permission[5].

programs.name: Senior Health Information and Insurance Education (SHIINE)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to all Medicare beneficiaries, those approaching Medicare, and their caregivers.
other_requirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary in South Dakota (or approaching Medicare eligibility), or caregiver of such a person.

benefits.value: Free, confidential, unbiased counseling including: understanding Medicare options (Parts A, B, D, Advantage, Medigap); plan comparisons and enrollments; Medicare appeals; fraud prevention education via SMP (Senior Medicare Patrol); Medicare billing issues; application assistance for low-income programs (e.g., Medicare Savings Programs like QMB/SLMB/QI, prescription assistance); referrals to other agencies; printed materials; one-on-one counseling. No fixed dollar amounts or hours specified.

methods: Phone - Western/Central South Dakota: 1-877-286-9072 or western_region@shiine.net
Phone - Eastern South Dakota: 1-800-536-8197 or eastern_region@shiine.net
Phone - Statewide toll-free: 1-888-854-5321
Website: https://www.shiine.net
Email: caitlin.christensen@state.sd.us
In-person: Contact regional coordinators or local counselors via phone/website for appointments
application.processing_time: No formal application processing; services provided via appointment scheduling, typically immediate upon contact.
forms: No specific forms required; counselors assist with any needed Medicare or low-income program applications during sessions.
documents: Medicare card, current insurance details, prescription lists, billing statements (recommended for personalized help, but not mandatory to start).

geography.restrictions: None; available to all South Dakota Medicare beneficiaries.
geography.regional_variations: Three regional coordinators (Western/Central, Eastern, Northeast) with dedicated phone/email contacts; local volunteer counselors vary by area; services consistent statewide but access via region-specific contacts.
offices_or_providers: South Dakota Department of Social Services, 700 Governors Drive, Pierre SD 57501-2291
Regional: Western SD (1-877-286-9072), Eastern SD (1-800-536-8197), Northeast (contact via statewide lines)

gotchas: Not a healthcare or financial aid program itself—provides education and assistance only, not direct payments or medical services.
Not affiliated with insurance companies; unbiased and free.
Contact region-specific numbers for fastest local service rather than statewide line.
Includes SMP for fraud prevention, which people may overlook.
Open enrollment periods (e.g., Oct 15-Dec 7 for next year changes) highlighted for timely action.
programs.data_shape_notes: No income/asset tests; eligibility tied solely to Medicare status; regionally coordinated with volunteer network; counseling-focused SHIP program, not benefits-paying.

South Dakota Respite Care Program

eligibility.age: No specific age minimum for the person receiving care; program serves children and adults[3][5]

## Key Distinctions for Families

**Which program applies to you?** The critical first step is determining which respite care program your elderly loved one qualifies for. If your loved one is elderly or a dependent adult (not specifically a child with developmental disabilities), you'll apply through **Adult Services and Aging**[1]. This program has asset limits ($30,000 for individuals, $35,000 for couples) but no income limits[1].

**What you actually get:** For elderly/dependent adults, respite care covers **companionship, meal preparation, light housekeeping, and help with personal hygiene**—but explicitly does not cover skilled nursing or medication administration[1]. Hours are capped at 210 per quarter (roughly 16 hours per week) with a daily maximum of 13 hours[1].

**The application process is straightforward but requires documentation:** You'll need a completed application form, documentation of your loved one's diagnosis from a physician or therapist, and a needs assessment completed by an Adult Services and Aging Social Worker[1][3]. Contact the Respite Care Program office in Pierre at **605-773-3438** (Monday–Friday, 8:00am–5:00pm CT) or visit in person at **3800 E. Highway 34, Pierre, SD 57501**[5].

**What people often miss:** The asset limit applies to the entire household regardless of whether one or both spouses need services[1]. Additionally, if your loved one requires skilled nursing care or total care, respite care may not be the appropriate program[1].

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Not more than 125% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL) established annually by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services[1][3]. Specific dollar amounts by household size are not provided in available sources, but the income calculation may treat a person with a disability as a family of one[4].
other_requirements: Must be unemployed at time of eligibility determination (or employed with notice of pending termination)[4]
Must be a resident of the state and county where DLR is authorized to operate SCSEP[1]
Must be eligible to work in the United States[2]
Must not have exceeded 48-month/4-year lifetime cap on SCSEP participation[4]

benefits.value: Part-time work training averaging 20 hours per week at whichever is highest—federal, state, or local minimum wage. Training typically lasts about 6 months before job placement assistance[3]

methods: In-person: Contact local SCSEP office (specific office locations and phone numbers not provided in available sources)
Online: Visit www.workworld.org (referenced as a resource)[2]
Mail: Not specified in available sources
application.waitlist: Waitlists may exist; enrollment occurs if eligible and no waiting list is present[3]
forms: Application form (specific form number/name not provided in available sources)

geography.restrictions: Applicant must reside in the state and county where DLR (South Dakota Department of Labor and Regulation) is authorized to operate SCSEP[1]. No requirement for length of residence prior to enrollment[1]
offices_or_providers: South Dakota Department of Labor and Regulation (DLR) administers the program[1][8]
Specific office locations not provided in available sources

gotchas: Income limits are based on 125% of Federal Poverty Level, which varies annually and by household size—families must verify current limits with their local office[1][4]
There is a 4-year lifetime participation cap; individuals cannot re-enroll after exceeding 48 months of cumulative participation[4]
Place of residence is required at enrollment but not at recertification[4]
Host agency assignment does not need to be in the participant's county of residence[1]
Priority is given to certain populations (age 65+, veterans, rural residents, disabled, homeless/at-risk, limited English/low literacy, low employment prospects, failed One-Stop System)[5]
programs.data_shape_notes: SCSEP is a federally funded program under Title V of the Older Americans Act with state administration. Benefits are fixed (not scaled by household size). The program emphasizes work-based training rather than direct financial assistance. Income eligibility is the primary barrier; specific dollar thresholds require annual verification with local offices.

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors

eligibility.income_limits: People 60 and older are often eligible regardless of income at providers like Dakota Plains Legal Services (DPLS), East River Legal Services (ERLS), and SD Law Help. For general low-income eligibility (which may apply if over 60 but still screened), limits are typically 125% of Federal Poverty Guidelines (ERLS, Access to Justice/A2J), up to 200% for seniors/elderly (A2J, ERLS exceptions, SD Law Help). Exact 2026 FPG dollar amounts not in sources; calculated by household size upon application.[1][2][3][4][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: Non-exempt assets may be considered at ERLS (details on what counts/exempts not specified). No asset limits mentioned for seniors at DPLS or statewide senior programs.[1][4]

Greatest economic and social need for DHS Legal Services for Vulnerable Older Adults.[5]
U.S. citizen or permanent legal resident (ERLS).[4]
Case must fit program priorities (e.g., elder abuse, long-term care, not all cases accepted).[1][2]

Specific senior cases: elder abuse, long-term care. Limited assistance; not all cases accepted. No fixed dollar amounts or hours; scope varies by resources and case merits.[1][2][5]

methods: Online: https://www.sdlawhelp.org/apply (serves DPLS, ERLS, Access to Justice).[6][7]
Phone: DPLS (check Areas Served page for offices); ERLS (605) 336-9230 or (800) 952-3015; Access to Justice 855-287-3510; Disaster hotline (605) 444-3719.[2][4][6]

documents: Proof of income; proof of residence; for permanent residents, official residence card (ERLS). Exact list determined upon intake.[4]

geography.restrictions: County-based: DPLS (western SD, check Areas Served); ERLS (33 eastern counties listed above); A2J statewide referral if others unavailable.[1][4][6]
geography.regional_variations: Different providers by region (DPLS east/west split? Areas Served page; ERLS eastern only). Availability varies by staff/resources.[1][4][7]

East River Legal Services (ERLS): http://erlservices.org
Access to Justice (A2J): State Bar program, statewide referral

Regional residency required; referral to other offices if outside area.[1][4]
Even if eligible, services limited by resources/staff; may get advice only, not full representation.[1][4]
Income calculated by gross household earnings; assets may factor in some programs.[4]
Apply to find out—encouraged even if unsure.[2]

eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; no financial eligibility requirements.

Complaint or issue must impact the health, safety, welfare, or rights of a resident or former resident.

benefits.value: Advocacy for residents' rights; complaint resolution; information and referral assistance; education on residents' rights; review of medical records; assistance with guardianship, medical/treatment issues, and facility concerns (e.g., staffing shortages); collaboration with facilities to resolve issues.

documents: None required

programs.name: Senior Companions of South Dakota

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits for clients receiving services. Volunteers (age 55+) must meet income guidelines: 1-person household $30,120; 2-person $40,380; 3-person $49,720; 4-person $60,000; add $5,140 per additional member.[1]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned for clients or volunteers.
other_requirements: Clients: disabilities considered, availability of other assistance, live in homes/apartments (especially rural areas).[2][5]
Volunteers: age 55+, able to serve 15-40 hours/week (average 10-15), healthy enough to provide assistance.[1][5]

benefits.value: Free companionship visits (no fee to clients): friendship, assistance with daily activities, light housekeeping, meal planning/preparation, playing games, reminiscing, transportation; respite for caregivers; support for recuperating/terminally ill/Alzheimer's families. Volunteers serve 2-4 clients, 15-40 hours/week; receive hourly stipend (~$2.65), mileage/transport, paid holidays/training/leave, insurance.[1][2][4][5][7]

methods: In-person/referral: Agencies like DayBreak Adult Day Care (Sioux Falls), Dow-Rummel Village, Good Samaritan Society locations.[2]

geography.regional_variations: Different regional offices/phone numbers (Sioux Falls vs. Rapid City); providers via Good Samaritan Society and referral agencies in Sioux Falls area.[1][2]
